coredata.dc:title: Thorn apple (Datura stramonium) extract acts as a sustainable corrosion inhibitor for zinc alloy in hydrochloric acid solutions

coredata.dc:description: The inhibition effectiveness of diverse concentrations of Datura Fruit Extract (DFE) on the corrosion of zinc in 0.05–0.1 M HCl solutions was examined by weight loss (WL), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), and surface analysis techniques. The mass loss method's results demonstrated that raising the DFE concentration improved the efficiency of the inhibition. The maximum inhibition efficiency was 99.40% at 2.0 g/L of DFE. In the temperature ranging from 303 to 333 K, the effect of temperature on the corrosion behavior while adding DFE was also investigated. A DFE extract had been adsorbed on the surface of the metal by Langmuir adsorption isotherm models. It was established that the DFE's activation parameters and negative Gibb's free energy show that rates of adsorption have been spontaneous. According to polarization analysis, DFE regulates both the cathodic and the anodic response. Images obtained using a SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope) reveal that the DFE reduces surface damage. The adsorption inhibition mechanism is supported by the AFM analysis findings. FT-IR (Fourier transform infrared) spectroscopy has been utilized to ascertain the functional group of the DFE. All of the applicable approaches' experimental findings are in good agreement.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inhibition effectiveness of diverse concentrations of Datura Fruit Extract (DFE) on the corrosion of zinc in 0.05–0.1 M HCl solutions was examined by weight loss (WL), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), and surface analysis techniques. The mass loss method's results demonstrated that raising the DFE concentration improved the efficiency of the inhibition. The maximum inhibition efficiency was 99.40% at 2.0 g/L of DFE. In the temperature ranging from 303 to 333 K, the effect of temperature on the corrosion behavior while adding DFE was also investigated. A DFE extract had been adsorbed on the surface of the metal by Langmuir adsorption isotherm models. It was established that the DFE's activation parameters and negative Gibb's free energy show that rates of adsorption have been spontaneous. According to polarization analysis, DFE regulates both the cathodic and the anodic response. Images obtained using a SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope) reveal that the DFE reduces surface damage. The adsorption inhibition mechanism is supported by the AFM analysis findings. FT-IR (Fourier transform infrared) spectroscopy has been utilized to ascertain the functional group of the DFE. All of the applicable approaches' experimental findings are in good agreement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most frequently used metals, zinc is used in a variety of industrial, domestic, and architectural purposes (Sithuba et al., 2022). Steel is primarily protected against corrosion by zinc, an active metal with a wide range of industrial uses. The zinc-coated steel components provide increased corrosion resistance, but when exposed to a humid environment, they corrode quickly and generate the white rust corrosion product (Ogoko et al., 2009). Galvanized materials often develop white rust, which makes the plated zinc materials unsuitable for industrial uses. Both alkaline and acidic solutions may cause zinc to corrode, however, corrosion is most pronounced in solutions with pH values between 6.0 and 12.5, where it is also relatively sluggish (Guruprasad et al., 2019). Zinc degradation is revealed by industrial processes including scale removal and acidic washing of zinc surfaces. Thus, the usage of inhibitors is required to safeguard the metal against corrosion (Gao and Liang, 2007). It is usual practice to use enough synthetic heterocyclic chemicals in hostile flow settings to protect metal surfaces (Desai and Kapopara, 2009, 2014). These molecules have a critical function in promoting the development of a film layer that is protective, which protects the surface of the metal from deterioration. Due to their high toxicity and substantial environmental risks, the usage of such corrosion inhibitors in the industry is restricted. These inhibitors are also costly and unfriendly to the environment. With increasing concerns about the toxicity of synthetic compounds and their environmental impact, there is a growing focus on finding eco-friendly alternatives for corrosion inhibition. Plant products offer a promising solution due to their biodegradability, renewability, affordability, non-toxicity, and ecological acceptance. However, studies on eco-friendly inhibitors for Zn corrosion inhibition in acid solutions have been very limited compared to other nonferrous metals. However, plant extract has shown remarkable effectiveness as an inhibitor against non-ferrous metal corrosion in acidic conditions (Khadraoui et al., 2016; Fouda et al., 2020; DI Njoku et al., 2019; Deng and Li, 2012; El-Katori and Al-Mhyawi, 2019; Nathiya and Raj, 2017; Méndez-Figueroa et al., 2022; Desai and Desai, 2023; Desai et al., 2023). The historical utilization of medicinal herbs to prevent corrosion in acid solutions on non-ferrous metal alloys serves as a testament to the potential of plant-based inhibitors (Mary Anbarasi and Divya, 2017; Kumpawat et al., 2012). As a consequence, the creation of fresh, environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors produced from plants has been a top priority for corrosion researchers (Zhang et al., 2021).

The current study aims to investigate the inhibitive efficacy of Datura fruit extract in HCl solutions of varying contents (0.05, 0.075, and 0.10 M). The effectiveness of the extract's inhibition will be evaluated using techniques, including mass loss measurements, Tafel polarization, SEM, AFM, and EIS. By assessing the inhibition efficiency of the Datura fruit extract, the research seeks to contribute to the understanding of its potential as a corrosion inhibitor, which may have practical applications in industries where HCl corrosion is a concern. The history and importance of Datura are briefly covered in the lines that follow before moving on with the experiment. Datura, also known as the Thron apple or Jimsonweed, is a genus of flowering plants that belongs to the family Solanaceae (Sayyed and Shah, 2014). In India, Datura is known as dhuttura Kanaka or Kanakahvya in Sanskrit, and kanaka means gold. Hence datura is known by all the names of gold. Because of this potent herb is extensively discussed in ancient Ayurvedic texts such as the Charak Samhita and Raj Nighantu (Vaidya, 1999). Datura is known for its effectiveness in treating various conditions, including enhancing stomach fire, aiding digestion, addressing skin disorders, stimulating appetite, relieving indigestion, rejuvenating the body, preventing nausea and vomiting, managing diabetic symptoms, preventing cough and cold, alleviating burning sensations, addressing respiratory ailments, improving complexion, and treating heart problems, and also supported by some scientists (Al-snafi, 2017; Alam et al., 2021).

The experiment involved using rectangular specimens measuring 5.0 cm × 2.0 cm x 0.1 cm, with a small hole of 0.5 cm diameter near one end, to assess the corrosion product of zinc. The visible surface area of every specimen was calculated as 0.2234 mg dm−2. To prevent atmospheric corrosion, all specimens were cleaned by buffing and wrapped in plastic bags before being used. Analytical reagent-grade chemicals were utilized in the experiment, guaranteeing great purity. Double distilled water had been utilized to make the test solutions, aiding in the experiment's ability to be conducted in a controlled atmosphere. The test specimen's chemical composition is displayed in Table 1 .

The Datura fruits with seeds utilized in this study were carefully collected and subjected to a thorough washing process to eliminate impurities, specifically dust. Subsequently, the fruits were air-dried in the open air at room temperature for 15 days. The dried Datura fruits with seed materials were then finely ground in a blender to ensure uniformity in particle size. Similarly, the fruits were dried and crushed into powder. A 25 g sample of the dry powder was extracted using 350 mL of distilled alcohol in a Soxhlet system for 5 h. Upon completion of the extraction, the ethanol was separated through fractional distillation, yielding an inhibitor solution. The crude extracts were stored in glass bottles in the refrigerator until further experiments were conducted. Inhibitor content varying from 0.4 to 2.0 g/L were prepared.
Utilizing a renowned Bruker spectrophotometer, the FTIR spectra of Datura fruit extract was captured and monitored across a comprehensive wavelength range spanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1.

The zinc specimens used in this experiment were immersed in three different HCl solutions with varied concentrations: 0.05 M, 0.075 M, and 0.10 M. The immersion process was conducted with and without datura fruit extract, and it lasted for 24 h at a room temperature of 303 K. After the 24-h immersion period, the specimens were cleaned using a solution containing 10% CrO3 and 0.2% BaCO3 for 2 min. The samples were then cleaned with the use of distilled water, cleaned again with the use of acetone, and dried with an air dryer. Experiments were done in triplicate for each instance to assure accuracy. The corrosion loss data were expressed in terms of milligrams per square decimeter (mg/(dm2. h)), and the corrosion loss's average value was given.

In the research, a zinc coupon was immersed in 230 mL of 0.05 M HCl solution containing an inhibitor content of 0.4–2.0 g/L at different temperatures: 303 K, 313 K, 323 K, and 333 K. Investigating how temperature affects corrosion rate was the goal. A thermostat assembly with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C had been utilized to regulate and maintain exact temperatures. It is worth noting that a temperature control accuracy within ±1 °C is generally considered acceptable according to Wesely (Wesley and Corrosion International Nickel Company, 1956) and ASTM (Am. Soc, 1950). The results of calculations to estimate parameters, such as the inhibition efficiency (ρ%), activation energy (Ea), adsorption-free energy (ΔG0 ads), and adsorption heat (Qads) are presented in Tables 2 and 3
.

The following formulae have been utilized for determining the loss of corrosion (CLuninhi) in the uninhibited system and the corrosion loss with inhibition (CLinhi):

In equation (1), the inhibitory effectiveness (ρ%) is computed as the percentage reduction in corrosion loss achieved by the inhibitor. Eq. (2) represents the surface coverage degree (θ) showing the percentage of the inhibitor-coated metal surface.

A sample was polarized in the electrochemical examination to change its current or potential, which was then recorded. 2 methods can be used for this purpose: DC (direct current) or AC (alternating current). For this specific study, Zinc metal specimens with a surface area of 1 cm2 were subjected to the corrosive solution. Electrochemical measurements were conducted at a temperature of 303 K using Galveostat/Poentiostat from CH-instruments (model CHI608C). A 3-electrode setup had been utilized for the measurement. In this setup, an additional electrode of platinum (Pt) has been immersed in a 100 mL solution of 0.05 M HCl, while Zinc metal has been utilized like a working electrode. An Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode. The Galveostat/Poentiostat automatically supplied the external current required for the investigation and the resulting potential changes. To analyze the electrochemical process occurring within the cell, Tafel curves were obtained, which represent a current-potential diagram. These curves illustrate the anodic and cathodic processes. It was possible to determine the anodic & cathodic Tafel constants by looking at the slopes of the polarization curve. At the corrosion potential, impedance measurements were made using a frequency range between 100 KHz and 10 mHz. 5 mV of signal amplitude perturbation was used. Potentiodynamic polarization experiments have been conducted in every possible range of 500–2000 mV at a scan rate of 1 mV/s.

For surface characterization, the specimens were exposed to a 24-h immersion in 230 mL of HCl, with the addition of Datura fruit extract at their respective optimum concentration (2.0 g/L). Subsequently, the specimens underwent rinsing and drying and were then subjected to surface morphological measurements. SEM imaging was performed using the FEI NOVA Nano FESEM 450 model at 1000X magnification, while AFM analysis was conducted utilizing the NT-MDT multimode AFM instrument. The AFM analysis specifically focused on a surface area of 1 cm2 to evaluate the topography and features of the specimens.

Inhibitor efficiency is a crucial parameter in the study of inhibiting processes, as it quantifies the capacity of an inhibitor to mitigate or lower the rate of a specific reaction. In this context, Fig. 1 provides a visual representation of how the inhibitor's concentration and acid concentration influence its efficiency in inhibiting process. The figure (Fig. 1) indicates that the inhibitive efficiency (p%) of the Datura fruit extract reduces with a rise in acid content when the inhibitor concentration is kept constant. This trend might be attributed to the increased aggressiveness of the acid environment at higher acid concentrations. The corrosive nature of the acid might overpower the inhibitive action of the Datura extract, leading to reduced efficiency. On the other hand, at constant acid content, the p % of the Datura fruit extract rises with the inhibitor content (Fig. 1). This finding indicates that higher content of the inhibitor results in better protection against the zinc metal.
Effect of temperature:

This research reveals the kinetic and thermodynamic factors related to the inhibition and adsorption processes by examining the impact of temperature on corrosion rates and inhibition efficiency. The interpretation of inhibitor adsorption type becomes clear with the use of these parameters. The experimental results, summarized in Tables 2 and 3, demonstrate a decrease in the inhibitory efficiency of Datura fruit extract as the temperature increases. To accurately quantify the effect, the activation energy (Ea) was computed using the Arrhenius equation (Eq. 3) (Arab, 2008; Poornima et al., 2010):
(3)

In the context of the mentioned equation (3), ‘P’ represents the corrosion loss of Zn metal, R is the universal gas constant, and T is the absolute temperature. The plot of log (P) against the reciprocal of absolute temperature (1/T) results in a straight line with a slope of Ea/R, allowing for the determination of Ea values for both the inhibition and corrosion process. Fig. 2 illustrates the Arrhenius plots for the zinc corrosion in a 0.05 M HCl solution, both in the presence and absence of various concentrations of Datura fruit extract. The observed rise in Ea in the presence of the inhibitor, compared to the blank solution, strongly indicates that the inhibitor is undergoing physical adsorption on the corroding Zn metal surface. Conversely, when Ea values remain unchanged or reduced in the presence of an inhibitor, it suggests chemisorption (Bentiss et al., 2005). Table 3 highlights the significant increase in Ea values after the introduction of Datura fruit extract, supporting the conclusion that corrosion inhibition of Datura fruit extract primarily occurs through physical adsorption. It is observed that the activation energy of corrosion reactions changes significantly when the concentration of corrosion inhibitors is higher (1.6–2.0 g/L) due to its role in the context of reaction kinetics and the collision theory. Therefore, by increasing the concentration of inhibitors, you provide more reactant molecules in a given volume, leading to higher frequency of collisions and a greater likelihood that these collisions will have energies surpassing the Ea. This increased collision frequency and energy facilitate a higher reaction rate.

Furthermore, additional thermodynamic parameters such as the enthalpy of the corrosion process (ΔH*) and the entropy (ΔS*), have been computed with the transition state equations (Eq. 4 & 5) (Putilova et al., 1960). The significant entropies obtained suggest that the activated complex form during the rate-determining step involves an association rather than a dissociation process. This observation recommends that a reduction in disordering occurs during the transition from reactants to the activated complex (Oguzie, 2007; Martinez and Stern, 2002).

The calculation of adsorption heat (Qads) was performed with Eq. (6). Where θ1, and θ2 values represent the surface coverage at temperatures T1 and T2, respectively. Table 4 indicates that all Qads values obtained are consistently negative, ranging from 1.40 to −213.5 kJ mol−1. These negative values unequivocally indicate the presence of adsorption, and consequently, the temperature-dependent inhibitory effectiveness (p%) decreases. Such patterns imply that temperature significantly impacts the inhibitor's ability to bind to metal surfaces, with increasing temperature leading to a reduction in the inhibitor's inhibitive effectiveness.

To evaluate the interaction between the Datura fruit extract and metallic surface, different adsorption isotherm models were examined. The Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm models were considered, with the Langmuir isotherm providing the best fit. Using the Langmuir adsorption isotherm Eq. (7) (Verma et al., 2016a), the adsorption constant (Kads) was assessed at various temperatures and the optimal concentration of Datura fruit extract. These adsorption constants provide quantitative information about the strength and extent of adsorption. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm plot in Fig. 3 illustrates the relationship between the adsorption capacity and the equilibrium content of inhibitors.

In Eq. (7), the parameter θ represents the degree of surface coverage, while C denotes the concentration of DFE in g/L. A higher value of Kads signifies a greater ability for adsorption. The calculated values of Kads at various temperatures can be found in Table 4.

Additionally, the adsorption standard free energy (ΔG0 ads) is calculated using Eq. (8), where the numerical value 995.65 represents the content of water in a solution, the unit of inhibitor concentration (g/L) and other symbols have their customary meanings (Fouda et al., 2015; Bentiss et al., 2009; Al-moubaraki and Al-rushud, 2017; Ganash and Alshelaly, 2022). In Table 4, the calculated Kads values correspond to significantly negative ΔG0 ads values for Datura fruit extracts, underscoring their substantial adsorption capacities (Hamdy et al., 2013; Mashuga et al., 2015; Loto et al., 2016). The elevated Kads values for Datura fruit extracts suggest a strong indication of adsorption onto the metallic surfaces (Verma et al., 2016a, 2016b).

The adsorption process of Datura fruit extract on the zinc surface is spontaneous and results in a stable adsorbed layer. The negative values of ΔG0 ads show the spontaneity of the process. Values of ΔG0 ads up to −20.0 kJ mol−1 recommends electrostatic interactions between the charged metal and the Datura fruit extract, indicating physical adsorption. More negative values of ΔG0 ads below −40.0 kJ mol−1 suggest charge sharing or transfer from the inhibitor molecule to the metal surface, indicating chemical adsorption (Ating et al., 2010; Ebenso, 2003). In the present research, the value of ΔG0 ads obtained for Datura extract adsorption on the zinc surface ranged from −10.87 to −19.87 kJ mol−1, which suggests that the adsorption process” primarily involves physisorption, a type of physical adsorption.

The study examined the impact of different content of Datura fruits on the polarization curves of Zinc (Zn) in a 0.05 M HC1 solution at 303 K. The corrosion parameters extracted from the polarization graphs, “including corrosion potential (Ecorr) anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes, corrosion current densities (icorr), and surface coverage (θ), are presented in Table 5 . Analysis of the table reveals that increasing concentrations of Datura fruit result in a consistent reduction in both the anodic and cathodic current densities, while the corrosion potential remains relatively unchanged (Ecorr). However, the possible change towards the anodic direction caused by the highest content of the inhibitor in its blank solution was only 26 mV in the 0.05 M HCl solution. This value is lower than the limit of ±85 mV typically utilized to classify inhibitors as either anodic or cathodic (Oguzie et al., 2007). Consequently, the Datura fruits extract is taken into account as a mixed-type inhibitor in its behavior in this experiment. Additionally, the addition of the Datura fruit extract decreases the values of both cathodic and anodic Tafel constants, indicating the extract's role as a mixed inhibitor. Further examination of Table 5, demonstrates that the surface coverage (θ) increases with higher inhibition concentrations. The Datura fruit extract's reported inhibitory effectiveness might be related to the components' adsorption on the surface of zinc. Adsorbed molecules form a shield that isolates the surface of the metal from the hostile medium, slowing the pace of corrosion. The following equation has been utilized to calculate the inhibitory effectiveness (ρ%) from polarization measurements:

In Eq. (9), the terms icorr(u) and icorr(i) refer to the current density of corrosion in the existence & absence of an inhibitor correspondingly. By projecting the Tafel lines to each corrosion potential, these values are calculated (Fig. 4 ).

Fig. 5 depicts Nyquist plots illustrating the behavior “of Zinc in HCl solution 0.05 M both without and with a varied concentration of Datura fruit extract. As the concentration of DFE increases, the plots exhibit an increase in the diameter of the semicircles. To analyze the impedance spectra, the experimental data were fitted using the EIS analyzer program to a simple equivalent circuit model presented in Fig. 6 . The frequency (f
max ), equivalent circuit model comprises the solution resistance (Rs), Warburg (W), and the double layer capacitance (Cdl) in parallel with the charge transfer resistance (Rct). The electrochemical parameters and the inhibition efficiency (p%) for Zn in 0.05 M HCl solutions with distinct contents of Datura fruit extract are displayed in Table 6 . Using measurements, the inhibitory effectiveness was determined using the following equation (Eq. 10) (Quraishi and Rawat, 2002):
(10)

Where Rct(i) and Rct(o) represent the charge transfer resistances in the existence and absence of the extract, correspondingly. The results specify that the Rct values for Zn increase as the content of Datura fruit extract rises. This growth raises the possibility that a shielding layer has formed on the surface of the electrode, serving as a barrier to the transmission of mass and charge (Şafak et al., 2012; Khaled, 2008; Behpour et al., 2010). This shows that the gradual replacement of water molecules on the surface by inhibitor molecules is the cause of the shift in Rct values. As the inhibitor molecules occupy the surface, they act as a barrier, reducing the number of active sites available for the corrosion reaction to occur. This decrease in available active sites hinders the corrosion process, resulting in higher Rct (Gonçalves et al., 2002). Furthermore, the capacitance of the double layer declines with increasing extract content on the Zn system. The Cdl represents the ability of the electrical double layer at the metal-electrolyte interface to store charges. The Cdl values are estimated from impedance value using the Nyquist plot, and it is calculated by using the formula (Eq. 11) (Galal et al., 2005):

In Fig. 7 a and b, it can be observed that the Bode curves display an ascending trend in the linear slope sections as the amount of” Datura fruits extract rise. This observation points to the presence of Datura fruit extract absorbate on zinc prior to oxidation during sliding processes (Haddadi et al., 2019). Moreover, the augmentation of phase angles signifies an improved surface coverage (Udensi et al., 2020).

The effective use of AFM for obtaining roughness statistics from various surfaces, particularly focusing on the zinc surface. The use of AFM as a method to study roughness is mentioned as becoming more common (Hakeem et al., 2014). Fig. 8 , shows “3-D AFM images of the surface of the zinc without and with datura extract. It shows the impact of an inhibitor (Datura fruits extract) on the zinc surface's roughness. It further notes that zinc surfaces in the corrosive medium can be reached at a greater scale of 237.0 nm. Fig. 8(a and b) show 3D AFM images of zinc at 303 K with and without Datura fruits extract. The absence of the inhibitor leads to moderate damage caused by the acidic solution dissolving the zinc metal (Fig. 8a), with a scale maximum of 237 nm. On the other hand, the existence of the DFE protection coating on the surface minimizes zinc roughness, effectively shielding it from corrosion, resulting in the highest scale of the inhibited zinc surface being 49.0 nm at its maximum (Fig. 8b) (Chaubey et al., 2018). Using AFM techniques, 3-D roughness data were obtained, and Table 7 presents the values of Rmax (maximum roughness), Ra (average roughness), Rq (root mean square roughness), skewness value, and roughness kurtosis for zinc surface with and without Datura fruits extract. It highlights that the larger surface roughness of zinc in uninhibited leads to higher Ra, Rq, and Rmax values. However, these values were significantly reduced by adding 2.0 g/L of Datura fruit extract, likely because of the oxide coating's dissolution. The Datura fruits extract makes the surface look more level, uniform, and consistent as a consequence (Fig. 8b). A good bearing surface with a negative skewness score indicates roughness measurements to evaluate the surface variations symmetry around the mean line (Rajesh Kumar and Subba Rao, 2012). As a statistical indication of the spike distribution above and below the mean” line, roughness kurtosis is also considered. Kurtosis values are favorable for spiky surfaces (higher than 3), less favorable for bumpy surfaces (less than 3), and neutral (around 3) for completely random surfaces in both systems, with and without inhibitor. Such findings suggest that the Datura fruit extract provides a suitable level of corrosion resistance.

Fig. 9 presents SEM micrographs of Zinc samples with and without 2.0 g/L of the investigated Datura extract. The absence of investigated extract (blank image) shows a surface that is heavily corroded and rough. However, the existence of datura extract leads to a notable reduction in zinc surface damage, transforming it into a much smoother surface.

The element composition of the Zn samples was investigated using EDX analysis before and after the addition of the studied inhibitor. The EDX analysis revealed significant insights into the variation that happens on the surface of the zinc. The EDX spectrum of the uninhibited Zn sample displayed notable peaks corresponding to zinc (Zn), oxygen (O), and carbon (C) atoms (Fig. 10 a). This observation indicates the presence of impurities or contaminants on the Zn surface. Upon comparing the elemental composition of the blank spectrum with the spectrum of the Zn sample in the presence of Datura extract (Fig. 10b), distinct differences were observed. Notably, the peak of Chlorine (Cl) and O were substantially reduced in the spectrum of the sample with the inhibitor. This reduction in Cl and O peaks indicates a reduction in the density of corrosion active sites on the Zn surface after the addition of Datura extract. This seems to hinder the formation of these active sites, which are typically involved in corrosion processes. The mass percentage of elements existing in the EDX spectrums were noted in Table 8 .

The corrosion process of Zn in the HCl electrolyte involves multiple cathodic reactions and anodic reactions. Regardless of the specific cathodic reaction, the anodic reaction always involves the oxidation of Zn, represented as
Z n
→
Z n
+ 2
+
2 e
−
. In the existence of hydrogen ion (H+) from the dissociation of HCl in the electrolyte, these ions can react with the free electrons in the Zn to produce hydrogen gas at the cathodic regions, and the reaction is represented as:
2 H
+
+
2 e
−
→
H 2
(Fontana, 1986).

Investigating various concentrations of Datura extract in an acid solution reveals its ability to retard Zn corrosion. The study reveals that Datura extract acts as an inhibitory system, blocking the Zn surface by adsorption. Both the inhibitors chemical structure as well as the nature of the metal play a crucial part in the process of adsorption (Sharma et al., 2013). Valuable data on the corrosion inhibition mechanism are derived from thermodynamic parameters, indicating a preference for physisorption type adsorption of Datura extract, where the Datura extract (effective lone pair electrons) may electrostatically adsorb on the Zn surface. However, a major constraint highlighted by Umoren et al. (2019) is that plant extract, like Datura, contains a multitude of heteroatoms, making it challenging to pinpoint a specific molecule accountable for the process of inhibition. Datura plants include major phytochemicals alkaloids, phenolic compounds, Tannins, and Flavonoids (Bagewadi et al., 2019) while seed contains Alanine, Glutamate, and Tyrosine (Fatima et al., 2015). Among the alkaloids present in Datura species, the major tropane alkaloids found in Datura are hyoscine and atropine are also present in different concentrations in various parts of the plant (Jakabová et al., 2012), Among the aforementioned molecules, contributing to increased inhibition efficiency. The capacity of these heteroatoms in the plant extract to contribute electrons is attributed to molecules adhering to Zn surfaces via a nonshared pair of oxygen atom electrons (Desai and Vashi, 2011). The high inhibitory efficacy, which increases extract adsorption on the metal surface, may be caused by the considerable electron delocalization in aromatic rings, planarity, and the existence of lone pairs of electrons on the ‘O' and ‘N' atoms. This is further confirmed by Fig. 11 of IR spectrum.

Fig. 11 describes the FTIR spectrum of the Datura fruit extract, highlighting various characteristic bands corresponding to specific functional groups. The key FTIR bands present in the Datura extract are in Table 9 . Analysis and interpretation of the FTIR data could lead to a better understanding of the properties and potential uses of the Datura fruit extract.

Based on the above results, the following conclusions can be drawn regarding the corrosion inhibition of Zn in 0.05 M HCl solution by Datura extract:
•
The results demonstrate that datura extract is highly effective in inhibiting the corrosion of Zn in 0.05–0.1 M HCl solutions. The inhibitor's presence significantly reduces the corrosive attack on the Zn surface. The inhibition efficiency of Datura extract increases with higher concentrations of the inhibitor.
•
The corrosion process of Zn is effectively inhibited by the adsorption of datura extract onto the metal surface.
•
The polarization curves indicate that Datura extract acts as a mixed-type inhibitor for Zn surface corrosion in the 0.05 M HCl solution.
•
EIS measurements reveal that the presence of datura extract increases the charge transfer resistance on the Zn surface.
•
The adsorption of datura extract on the Zn surface from 0.05 M HCl solutions follows the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms. The adherence to these isotherms suggests that the adsorption occurs in a monolayer, leading to a uniform coverage of the metal surface by the inhibitor.
•
3D-AFM and SEM images provide visual evidence confirming the formation of a protective layer on the Zn surface.
•
EDX analysis observation aligns with the previous findings from gravimetric and electrochemical experiments, confirming that the corrosion of Zn is indeed reduced after the addition of the Datura extract.

Adarsh Patel: Resources, Data curation.
